nativeRange.habitat: First-order plateau streams and headwater tributaries at 350–550 m elevation; sandy to silty substrate with aquatic macrophytes, marginal vegetation, and seasonal water-temperature swings from ~15°C (winter) to ~27°C (summer)

decorPreferences: leaf litter or driftwood for cover
dense aquatic plants or Java moss

diet.notes: Foraging bottom-dweller; accepts high-quality sinking micro-pellets and wafers as a staple. Supplement regularly with small live or frozen foods — bloodworms (chironomid larvae), Tubifex, micro-worms, brine shrimp nauplii. Will graze on biofilm and organic detritus. Should not be relied upon to subsist on tank leftovers. Feed small amounts at least once daily; a second evening feeding is beneficial.

goodWith: small tetras (e.g., ember tetras, neon tetras)
other small Aspidoras or Corydoras species
avoidWith: fast/boisterous tankmates that outcompete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Aspidoras fuscoguttatus; verify availability and license before uploading.

summary: A diminutive armored catfish (~4 cm) from high-altitude plateau streams of the upper Paraná basin, Brazil. Sandy-bodied with brown-spotted patterning, it is a peaceful shoaler that tolerates cooler temperatures than most corydorads. Intermediate care due to water-quality sensitivity and the need for conspecific groups.
careNotes:
Aspidoras fuscoguttatus belongs to the distinct genus Aspidoras (not Corydoras), identifiable by two cranial fontanels versus one in Corydoras. Wild populations inhabit first-order plateau streams at 350–550 m elevation in northwestern São Paulo State, Brazil, where seasonal temperatures range from about 15°C in winter to 27°C in summer — making this species noticeably more cool-tolerant than the majority of tropical corydorads. In the aquarium a range of 18–26°C is appropriate; temperatures at the lower end can actually be beneficial for long-term health and spawning conditioning.

A group of at least 6 individuals is strongly recommended; smaller groups show increased stress and reduced foraging activity. The tank must have fine sand — coarse or sharp gravel will abrade the delicate barbels and facial tissue, leading to infections. Water quality must be maintained carefully: perform 20–25% water changes weekly and ensure gentle, well-oxygenated flow. This species is copper-sensitive; avoid all copper-based medications and copper-bearing fertilisers.

Aspidoras fuscoguttatus is frequently confused with Corydoras paleatus and other spotted small corydorads in the trade; the narrower body profile, smaller adult size, and two cranial fontanels of Aspidoras distinguish it. It is rarely seen commercially and can be difficult to source. The species is diurnal and an active forager, frequently seen picking through the substrate and grazing on plant roots and biofilm.
breedingNotes: Breeding follows the typical corydoradine pattern. Condition a group of 2–3 females and at least 1–2 males with high-quality live and frozen foods for several weeks. Spawning is typically triggered by a large (30–40%) partial water change with cooler, soft water — mimicking the seasonal rains and temperature drops of the natural habitat. Eggs are laid in small clusters on plant leaves, the tank glass, or fine-leaved plants; adults provide no parental care and will consume eggs if not separated. Incubation is approximately 4–6 days at 22–24°C. Fry are tiny and should initially be fed infusoria or commercial micro-fry food, graduating to micro-worms and baby brine shrimp nauplii within the first week. Fry are sensitive to water-quality fluctuations; frequent small water changes with aged water are essential.

primary.name: FishBase — Aspidoras fuscoguttatus
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and biological reference. Confirmed: max size 3.8 cm SL, temperature 22–25°C, pH 5.5–6.8, hardness up to 12 dH, upper Paraná River basin, Brazil; Siluriformes / Callichthyidae / Corydoradinae; described by Nijssen & Isbrücker 1976; IUCN Least Concern; diurnal, benthopelagic, facultative air-breather.

additional.name: SciELO Brazil — Ecology of a stream from upper Paraná River basin inhabited by Aspidoras fuscoguttatus
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed ecological field study. Key data: wild temperature 15–27°C seasonally, altitude 350–550 m (São Paulo plateau), first-order tributary streams, fine sandy/silty substrate, near-neutral pH, shallow depth (0.04–0.23 m), air-breathing in hypoxic conditions, associated macrophytes and macroinvertebrate fauna. Confirms cool-tolerance and high-altitude plateau habitat.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Aspidoras fuscoguttatus
additional.notes: Cross-reference for common names (Darkspotted catfish, Brown-point shield skin longirostris), max size ~4 cm, temperature 22–25°C, pH 5.5–6.8, hardness 12 dGH, diet (worms, benthic crustaceans, insects, plant matter), egg-scattering with no parental care, note that species is difficult to distinguish from related taxa in the trade.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Aspidoras pauciradiatus (genus husbandry reference)
additional.notes: Used for Aspidoras-genus husbandry norms: peaceful and gregarious, group of 4–6 minimum, fine sand or smooth gravel substrate, sinking foods plus live/frozen, temperature 20–27°C, pH 6.0–7.0, spawning triggered by cool water change. Also confirms Aspidoras distinguished from Corydoras by two cranial fontanels.

additional.name: Checklist — New records of Aspidoras fuscoguttatus from the São Francisco river basin
additional.notes: Documents range extension of A. fuscoguttatus into the São Francisco river basin, Brazil — expanding known distribution beyond the upper Paraná basin. Supports multi-basin South American plateau distribution.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; spawning triggered by large cool water change mimicking rainy-season conditions. Eggs laid on plant leaves or glass in small batches; adults provide no parental care and must be separated post-spawn. Fry are tiny and require infusoria or commercial micro-fry food initially.
